speaker: interviewer
text: --two with Paul Goldstein. So you were left living with your mother and brother, and the address where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was number 1, and then in Dutch one would say [DUTCH], which means, street of justice. Because at the end of our streets was the Palace of Justice, the courts and everything. So that was a corner-- corner of the-- of the Road [? to Malin, ?] Brussels, and [DUTCH] right in the heart of Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother able to manage the salon herself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no, no. At that point-- at that point, I recall, your-- the-- all the Jewish businesses were closed. You were not allowed to-- the moment they started deportation of the men, first-- men always first-- the-- all the Jewish businesses were-- were closed, and-- and confiscated, in a way. Fortunately we had done quite well, and we had saved some money. So-- so we were living there not knowing what was going to happen, naturally devastated by the fact that our father and my mother's husband were gone-- was gone. And we didn't know what tomorrow was going to bring. It was a total--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got letters, though, from your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it so happened that the-- the Jewish community, through its central organizational body, was able to establish some contact with the people in the labor camps. These were labor camps. Matter of fact, I do recall, some how, that the name of the camp where my father was situated was a camp called Dannes Camir. No, whether that's spelled D-A-N-N-E-S, I think so, Camir, C-A-M-I-R, which was one of the camps that helped build the fortifications for the Germans on the French coast, in the Atlantic French coast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were labor camps, not-- not extermination camps. And so there was contact, with money and bribing some people, and couriers, it was possible to take letters, and sometimes a small parcel, to the Jewish committee's headquarters, and then they would see to it that these would reach, and vice versa. So there was some communication at that time. But this was in the-- What do you remember that your father wrote?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall receiving from my father, but I do recall sending this article-- no, maybe he sent back-- it wasn't a long period of-- of exposure to this. We're talking here-- the episode we're entering into, itself lasted maybe four to six weeks, so there wasn't much time to establish patterns. But the pattern that did-- we did have that at least once a week, we used to go from where we lived, which was in the gentile side, to the heart of the very, very, almost Jewish ghetto side, where most of the Orthodox Jews lived. And-- and that's where also the office of the Jewish Council was. And that's where we used to take whatever we were sending to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was to cross the park in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there's a-- there's a nice central park in the city of Antwerp, and we were-- where we lived, and at our business in the non-Jewish part, was on one side of the park, and the Jewish was-- area was exactly on the opposite side of the park.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about, there was one particular day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yes. [SIGHS] New chapter. Very bad memories on that particular day. On that particular day, my mother, and my brother, and I walked towards that office where we're going to deliver what we want to send to my father. And as we left the park and crossed the boulevard along the park, which I believe is called the Quinten [? Matsijslei, ?] Quinten Matsijs, I think, was a famous artist, or painter. All of the streets in the area were named after the Flemish painters. We stopped dead in our tracks because right in front of us, we saw a horrible scene. The-- we saw German officers, in beautiful shiny uniforms and big shiny boots, on the street, pick up, grab whoever had the star-- that's what the purpose of wearing the star, star of-- and just pick the people off the street and pushed them in those trucks. And just as we stood there ready to cross into that area, the picture that will always be my mind was of a little old lady, little old woman, tiny, with a shawl over her head. I don't remember what day of the week it was. And-- and she was kind of, her hands folded, on her knees begging to be let go because her family was waiting. And I remember that they just-- she was very small, and he was very big and just grabbed her, and literally threw her into the truck with the other people. And when my mother saw that, she immediately made an about-turn. And we went back to out home. And that's where we knew, that's when we saw the naked truth of what this was all about. That was the day of revelation. And-- so Mother realized that it was very dangerous to circulate, and especially with children because wherever there were-- we noticed, we had noticed or heard that, if you're-- if you're caught [INAUDIBLE] with their children, there would be no-- no two ways about it, we'll just be deported. There were all kind of rumors as to what happened to those people. There were always rumors. People being optimistic by nature always hoped for the best, and ignore rumors that are bad news.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my Mother-- also they started to go to houses, because now they had this list of people. So we heard that so-and-so had been picked up, and so-and-so had been picked up, and not necessarily restricted to the typically Jewish center. So my mother then started to have us stay with Gentile friends that knew us from our business and stay a few days here, a few days there. We were afraid to stay in our own home. So we basically not return home other than once in a while to pick something up. We stayed with Gentile people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the rumors were such, that it was dangerous to stay with children, so my mother decided she had to do something. She had to organize ourselves to go into permanent hiding. From [INAUDIBLE] we picked up and deported god knows where. She was a very smart and courageous lady, and she thought on her feet, literally.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had-- when we had this beauty salon, we knew a couple of-- a Christian couple that were actually ran a placement service, like a personnel service. So they always supplied us-- whenever we needed someone in the salon, male or female, hairdressers or barbers, or haircutters, we-- they used to supply us with those people. So we knew them. They had no children. And they were already-- it's hard for me, thinking back to judge how old they are, because when you're nine years old everybody over 30 looks old. But I think they must have been, in my opinion, in their mid '40s. That's only-- I'm adjusting for the-- for the tunnel vision I have because of my age, but otherwise I would thought they would be much older. But I would say in their mid '40s.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, as I said, they had no children, so Mother negotiated with them. She said, how would you like to-- also, don't forget that they did business with our-- our place was closed. I don't know how, whether they did a lot of business with non-Jewish enterprises, but the business wasn't flourishing.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother said, look we have money, and we don't know how long this is going to last. But how would you-- how-- how would you like to move to a-- to the countryside, rent a little cottage, and take on my children under your name and-- and-- and hide them? And keep them, and lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm going to go in hiding myself, and I will keep you supplied for rental money and whatever it costs on ongoing basis, from my hiding place. And this is what happened. They found-- they had relatives. Their own-- the lady, the-- the-- that I'm talking about, she had her own siblings and parents in a small village south of the city of Antwerp, on the highway to Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called, Hove, H-O-V-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Roggeman, R-O-G-G-E-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jose, which is short for Josephine. His name was Caesar-- Caesar. Caesar and Jose Roggeman. Catholic people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for a few days while they were organizing the little cottage that they rented, which was in a little side street, very quiet side street, was more of a path than a street, although it had that name, it was called the [? Kasteelstraat, ?] Castle Street, because at the corner of the street, in the highway, there was an old castle, and a big piece of land and-- it was number 12-- it was right along the rail tracks. Through our front window, we could see the tracks of the railroad from Brussels to Antwerp. And we were number 12. 12 [? Kasteelstraat ?] in Hove was the address.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a few days we stayed with their relatives until-- until the-- the house was properly set up. The-- I recall the first night that we were separated from my mother. My mother gave her whatever kids belongings were, and then she found a place in Brussels, went in hiding herself, and then we then went to Hove. And that would have been, best of my recollection, I would say September of 1942, give or take. Probably September 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember parting from your mother at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember saying goodbye. I remember us taking the train, it was one train stop from another suburb called, [INAUDIBLE] it means, old god. It was another suburb-- there were stations along the-- for every village on the way, so it was one station stop from where we-- we-- we met to go to this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember that the first night that we slept away from home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother was very, very-- he was much younger. He was-- he was-- he had just turned six years old, and I was-- I was three and 1/2 years older, so I was the protector, you see. I remember when we went into that strange bed in that strange house of these, of the relatives of the Roggemans, my brother asked me, he says, Paul, when are we gonna see Mother again? When are we gonna go home again? He asked me that. What did you call your mother? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- mama.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mama.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Flemish, mama would be, mama with the accent-- with the accent on the second syllable, mama. Mama and papa. So, when we're going to see mama again? And then I told-- as a matter of fact, I-- I grew up very quickly in those days because of-- because of the circumstances-- and I said, well, as soon as the war is over, we will go home. And he says, how long is that going to be? [CRYING] And I says, oh, not more than a year. I had to say something. Not more than a year. That, I remember, that was our first night away from home. That was the-- so my father was in this camp in France, my mother was hidden in Brussels, and we were hidden, living and hiding in this small village of Hove under the name, Roggeman. From there on, I was Paul Roggeman and my little brother was Rene Roggeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go to school in Hove?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because the Jewish schools were closed. And we were just lucky to have escaped the rounding up of the people. So there was no question at that time-- at that time of going to school. Although something happened with a school, a private school a few months later, but not right away. What was interesting was that I recognized quite a few children from the [INAUDIBLE] school in the same village. Unfortunately, the-- it was very hard for them to pass for Christians because of their language problem. Well, it wasn't a problem until then, but it became a problem. This was purely a Flemish-speaking village, and those children belonged to homes, were either French or Yiddish, and because of the Yiddish-- French with a Yiddish accent, or whatever-- very little Flemish was spoken. So that gave them-- gave them automatically away. As a matter of fact, I remember that a few months later when the Roggemans, under my mother's instructions, who supplied her money, had found a private school run by not-- official school we couldn't go to-- state school. Private school run by-- was a French school, it was called, Institut Les Abeilles, the bees, and it was about a few kilometers away from where we were living near the city hall of this other suburb called, [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and-- and then-- and we were, they accepted us. Matter of fact, a very interesting-- they knew that we were Jewish, but they were very patriotic people because that was a private French school, and the-- the principal had been a very patriotically lady, so she had the French flag flying on the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans told her that she had to take the French flag down, and had to have a swastika, German flag in all schools and buildings, but you could not-- you could not show any flag other than the German flag. She had been very patriotic, refused to take it down. So she was, although she was a Christian herself, she was deported and spend five years in a labor camp. So her daughter, [? Mrs. Paulette, ?] took over the school, running of the school. So she was very, very anti the authorities, and conducive to-- to-- to-- to let Jewish kids come in, as long as there was money to pay for them. So the point I'm trying to make is, so we walked on foot every morning to that school from where we were, and on the way, I fell in step with a local boy. I would say he was about 12, 13 years old, maybe even more, much bigger than we were. So-- because we walked the same way for whatever reason, under the same direction, we-- we started having conversations. How long was that walk? How far?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time it look much further than it really was, but I-- I would put it in-- in I would say it was about four or five kilometers still. But he didn't go the whole way with us because I remember at the end of a certain, one kilometer, he turned the other way to go to his school, and we kept going. We would-- we could have gone by train also, but there was more risk, and also it was a question of money. But the point was that he was bragging to us how he had turned in Jewish kids. Because the Germans had issued rewards, there was a reward of 2,000 Belgian francs for turning in any Jewish person you knew to be in hiding. In-- in Canadian dollars, at the exchange at the time, I would have said-- I would say that, at that time that would have been the equivalent of about $40 then, which in-- in-- in 1942-- Ironically, that's the right word, the population at large also was not allowed to-- to keep radio sets. So shouldn't be able to tune in to foreign radios, like BBC from London was beaming broadcast, following news from the free world, so the Germans, with their propaganda machine, didn't want anybody to be influenced by-- by the BBC. So-- so there were edicts out, they were posted everywhere, that anybody who was hiding a Jew, or having such a radio, would be subject to very stiff penalties. If you were-- if you could turn in anyone who was hiding a Jew, or that you knew someone to have such a radio set, there would be a reward of 2,000 francs. 2,000 francs for a radio, and 2,000 francs for a Jewish person. So that was the value of a Jew in German currency to the local population. And this young chap that was supposed to be a friendly co-walker was bragging every time he turned in Jewish children, to me. And I naturally-- I'd been warned by my parents and by the Roggemans to be as reticent as possible, never to talk to strangers, never to volunteer any information, and just to play the part of being good little Catholic boys, named Roggeman. And I realized the danger of reacting so it was very, very frustrating to have to hear this, and to realize how-- how bad this was, and have to act as if I-- as if I thought it was a great thing that he was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that Mrs. Roggeman took you to be baptized.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A little bit later, I would say about six months later, Mrs. Roggeman thought we'd be better off if we were baptized, so she took us to local priest of Hove. I remember we went to his house, and I remember us walking in, the three of us, Mrs. Roggeman, my brother, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she says, well, I have these Jewish kids here with me, and I want them to be baptized. So he says, where are their parents? Well, their parents-- father's been deported, and the mother I don't know. Oh, he says, I'm very sorry, but I won't baptize these children without the permission of their parents. Quite interesting. So as a result of his position, we were not baptized at the time. They didn't take it beyond that. Did they take you to Catholic church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We went to mass. We were-- we were-- we were forced, literally forced, in spite of anything, to-- to-- to behave as good Christians. So we were taught the Christian prayers, the Hail Mary and the other prayers, and we went to mass every Sunday to-- to the other small village of [INAUDIBLE], not too far away, and went to church on a regular basis. And we prayed the Christian prayers before and after meals. They were very Catholic. As a matter of fact, I didn't mind going to that church because that church had some kind of a-- of a grotto, a little altar to some miraculous-- miracle-performing saint, and there was a little fountain there with-- with-- with water, and since we were really deprived of food and drink, it was an opportunity to quench our thirst, so that was a good side of going to church.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the deprivation at the Roggemans.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, it turned out that the Mrs. Roggeman, not so much Mr. Roggeman, turned out to be a very vicious anti-Semitic and very sadistic person. And she turned our experience into a real hell. And anything, it was a-- we were there for over two years, they were two years of starvation, hard work, and beatings, on nonstop basis. Plus slurs and insults all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- first of all, we were starved, like we were-- the-- these people were pretty well off because with the money that they received from my mother, and it was a nice little house, it had a vegetable garden, where just about every possible vegetable was grown. And the-- Mr. Roggeman worked at the plant that was making parts for the German air force, called the Erlae plant, E-R-L-A-E, big plant near Antwerp, and brought home quite a bit of food from there, for own consumption. He would have gladly shared it with us, but she won't let us have any of it. So they lived quite well. When we went to school, for instance-- because we went to school for a very short time for a reason that I'll explain in a moment-- so we went to school. We would get, I would say, half a dozen slices, fairly thinly sliced bread, with a little bit of jam put on it, and that was our food supply for the day. We used to had it finished by the time we got to school, and there was nothing else to eat. And they wouldn't get any other food during the day. We were also not allowed to touch the-- the-- the water supply in the house so-- so we were always-- the one overriding feeling was always, always, in hunger pangs and thirst. This I remember very vividly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we-- we-- we scavenged. We-- we became little thieves. We used to go-- there were some-- some cottages from people who lived in the city, and there were some fruit trees on season. I used to go climb through the hedges and over the fences. My little brother used to stand guard to see if anybody was coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would steal fruit, or in the fields, steal raw beets, and turnips, potatoes. And-- and whenever we were send to do the shopping for them, because we did the shopping, and the wood cutting, and take laundry to the laundry-- laundry-- not mats, but to the laundry places, which was always a few kilometers away. We did all that work. Whenever went to a bakery, or to a [? food ?] store, before we-- we used to swipe whatever we could, piece of bread, piece of candy. And this is how we, more or less-- but, in the meantime, our health deteriorated badly. We were pretty-- in good shape when we started. But I would say within a couple of years we had-- we would look like-- like the pictures of the people that you see coming out of concentration camps, gaunt and-- and-- and like skeletons, and full of infections and warts, and so forth. That was the starvation part.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that you would dig up vegetables like turnips, did you have any way of cooking them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, everything was raw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would just eat them raw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, everything-- no way of cooking. And once in awhile, we got caught stealing. And then the people that we-- didn't realize who we were, so they would come and tell, complain to the Roggemans that we were caught in their garden stealing something. So naturally after-- this was always-- the-- the beatings, and after that were very, very fierce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who beat you, Mr.--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Roggeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Roggeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Mrs. Roggeman. Never mister, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she beat you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, hands-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] well, to give you-- give you one the many examples, which is, again, it was unforgettable and very traumatic for me to even think about, is, at the height of that period, I do recall that one day my little brother was sent to the cellar to bring back some potatoes. So he went to the cellar, and as I said, we were always, always thirsty. We were so thirsty that-- also, the hygiene was really poor. For instance, in our bedrooms, it was an upstairs bedroom, there was a pail for us to urinate through the night. Because it was always cold, so we couldn't go anywhere-- to the washrooms downstairs. And that pail in the morning was emptied, and fresh water was put in, but it was never scrubbed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a while there was about, I would say, a good 1/2 inch of acid, uric acid, covering the inside of the-- of the pail, the bucket. And yet, this is the-- from that bucket that we drank the water in the night because we were so thirsty.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so because of the thirst my brother went-- little brother, who at that time that I'm talking about was six years old-- went to the cellar to pick up those potatoes. He spotted a bottle of beer. Belgians are big beer drinkers, they have these one liter bottles of beer. I [? understand they're ?] brown bottles. So he opened the bottle and took a sip. Unfortunately, it didn't contain beer, it contained ammonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he let out a phenomenal scream because he completely burned his throat and everything in his esophagus. So when we heard that scream, we all rushed down, including myself. And instead of helping the kid, get over his pain, doing something, when Mrs. Roggeman [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saw that he had violated the rules, and try to drink-- although he couldn't drink because it wasn't even beer, she-- she went into a fury, and she picked up a-- a poke, a metal poke that was lying there, because of the firewood that was there, and she smacked him right over the head, and really-- and just kind of split his skull. The blood just poured out, and the kid-- she almost killed in front of my eyes. I-- [CRYING] most horrible thing because I couldn't help him. You couldn't help him.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was so terrible. So that's an example. [CRYING] My brother, I must say, has never fully recovered from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said he has the scar till today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the scar is there, but the mental scars are even worse because my brother has never been able to-- to lead a normal life. That's all I can say. Had a very, very tragic life after that. Another episode--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take a short break.
